cards.front: How does the note define marginal stability?
cards.back: A system is marginally stable if its impulse response h(t) remains bounded for all time.

cards.front: Does marginal stability require h(t) to go to zero?
cards.back: No. It only requires h(t) to stay finite. The response may keep oscillating forever.

cards.front: What kind of imaginary-axis poles are allowed in marginal stability?
cards.back: Only non-repeated imaginary-axis poles are allowed.

cards.front: Why are repeated imaginary-axis poles dangerous?
cards.back: They introduce terms such as t cos(omega t), so the amplitude grows with time and the system becomes unstable.

cards.front: What does a simple pole at j omega produce?
cards.back: It produces a constant-amplitude oscillation, which is bounded but non-decaying.

cards.front: What immediately makes a system unstable?
cards.back: Any pole in the right half plane, or any repeated pole on the imaginary axis.

cards.front: What is the difference between asymptotic stability and marginal stability?
cards.back: Asymptotic stability means the response decays to zero. Marginal stability means it stays bounded but may not decay.

cards.front: Why is multiplicity important in pole classification?
cards.back: Because repeated poles add polynomial factors like t, which can turn a bounded oscillation into an unbounded one.

cards.front: Give a pole pattern for a marginally stable system.
cards.back: A typical example is one with poles at -1 and plus/minus j2, where the imaginary-axis poles are simple.

cards.front: Why do engineers prefer poles comfortably inside the left half plane?
cards.back: Because decay gives robustness. Marginally stable systems are sensitive to modelling error and can easily drift into instability.
